ux_issues.description: Shared view booking data is minimal (only type, title, provider). Dates, confirmation codes, guest counts, and hotel details are omitted. While this may be intentional for privacy, it limits the usefulness of shared trips for collaborators who need to see logistics.
ux_issues.suggestion: Consider including non-sensitive booking fields (check-in/check-out dates, location) in the shared view, while omitting confirmation codes and payment details.

ux_issues.description: Usage endpoint reported used=1 after 2 SendMessage calls (1 selection + 1 planning). The selection mode message may not count toward the daily limit, but this is not documented and could confuse users checking their quota.
ux_issues.suggestion: Document which message types count toward the daily limit, or make the usage counter match the actual number of SendMessage calls.

usefulness_evaluation.narrative: The trip sharing lifecycle works correctly end-to-end. Token revocation is properly enforced (old tokens return 404 after unshare), re-sharing generates new unique tokens, and old tokens remain invalid after re-share. The shared view includes full itinerary content (21 items across 7 days) and booking summaries. No privacy leaks detected (no user IDs, tokens, or emails in shared view). The AI created a comprehensive 7-day Japan itinerary with 21 well-structured items covering Tokyo, Hakone, and Kyoto.
